summary: Fowler proposes a method for measuring technical debt by asking teams to estimate how long a feature would have taken in a clean system versus actual effort, with the difference representing 'interest' paid. He acknowledges the technique has flaws—estimates are subjective and based on imaginary states—but argues the visibility it provides to non-technical staff may justify the overhead. The approach can inform decisions about paying down debt by comparing interest payments to the estimated principal cost of cleanup. He suggests capturing these estimates during retrospectives rather than forward-looking planning may be more practical.
key_points: Technical debt interest can be estimated as the difference between actual effort and effort in an ideal clean system
This measurement technique is inherently subjective since it's based on an imaginary system state
The value lies in making technical debt visible to non-technical stakeholders
Comparing accumulated interest to principal cost can inform whether to pay down specific debt
Retrospectives may be a better time to capture these estimates than forward planning
money_quotes: Sadly technical debt isn't like financial debt, so it's not easy to tell how far you are in hock (although we seem to have had some trouble with measuring the financial kind recently).
The statement of how long it would have taken on a clean system is an estimate based on an imaginary state - so is difficult to make objective.
The result may help project a picture of the state of the code-base in a way that's visible to non-technical staff.
I spent an extra day on this feature because of the bad state of the flipper module.
Comparing interest payments with the principal may help inform a decision about whether to pay off the principal.
Doing this estimate against recent completed work may be easier than forward estimates against future stories.
key_insight: While measuring technical debt is inherently subjective, estimating the 'interest' paid on each feature can make the cost visible enough to inform decisions about cleanup investments.
